---
title: "cnefetools: Access and Analysis of Brazilian CNEFE Address Data in R"
abstract: >
  The \pkg{cnefetools} package provides programmatic access to the 2022
  Brazilian National Address File for Statistical Purposes (CNEFE), the
  geocoded address register of approximately 100 million addresses enumerated
  during the 2022 Brazilian Census and published by the Instituto Brasileiro de
  Geografia e Estatística (IBGE). The package offers four main capabilities:
  downloading and caching CNEFE data for any of Brazil's approximately 5,570
  municipalities; aggregating address counts to H3 hexagonal grids or
  user-supplied polygons; computing a suite of land-use mix indices---including
  the Bidirectional Global-centered Balance Index (BGBI) first introduced by
  \citet{pedreirajr2025}---directly from the geocoded address data; and
  performing dasymetric interpolation of census tract variables to finer
  spatial units using CNEFE dwelling points as ancillary data. A dual-backend
  design uses DuckDB as the default engine, reading CSV data directly from
  cached ZIP archives through the \emph{zipfs} extension and performing spatial
  joins and H3 cell assignment in-process, with speedups of up to 13$\times$
  over a pure-R fallback for large cities (H3 resolution 8 benchmark). A
  persistent user-level cache eliminates repeated downloads across R sessions.
  The package is available on CRAN.
author:
  - name: Jorge Ubirajara Pedreira Junior
    affiliation: Universidade Federal da Bahia
    address:
    - Escola Politécnica
    - Salvador, Bahia, Brazil
    url: https://pedreirajr.github.io/website/
    orcid: 0000-0002-8243-5395
    email: jorge.ubirajara@ufba.br
  - name: Bruno Mioto
    affiliation: Universidade Estadual de Maringá
    address:
    - Departamento de Estatística
    - Maringá, Paraná, Brazil
    email: brunomioto97@gmail.com
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
date_received: ~
description: |
  Access and analysis tools for the 2022 Brazilian CNEFE address data.
output:
  rjtools::rjournal_web_article:
    self_contained: yes
    toc: no
bibliography: cnefetools.bib
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  eval = FALSE
)
```

## Introduction

Address-level data are a fundamental resource for urban and regional
analysis. They enable fine-grained characterisation of land use, population
distribution, and the spatial organisation of economic and social
activities. Unlike parcel boundaries or building footprints---which often
require proprietary sources---geocoded address registers are administrative
by-products of census enumeration, making them publicly available in several
countries.

Brazil is home to the fifth-largest population in the world, with
approximately 215 million inhabitants spread across 5,570 municipalities
\citep{ibge2023}. The 2022 Brazilian Census, conducted by the Instituto
Brasileiro de Geografia e Estatística (IBGE), produced an exceptionally rich
by-product: the Cadastro Nacional de Endereços para Fins Estatísticos (CNEFE,
National Address File for Statistical Purposes). This dataset records the
geocoordinates and functional category of approximately 100 million addresses
across the entire country, making it arguably the most comprehensive
publicly available geocoded address dataset for any country in the Global
South.

Despite its potential, the CNEFE presents significant barriers to use. The
data are distributed as individual ZIP-compressed CSV files---one per
municipality---via the IBGE FTP server, with no unified API. Each file is
large (often tens of megabytes), semicolon-delimited, and encoded in Latin-1.
Spatial operations on these files---assigning points to grid cells, computing
land-use mix, or matching them to census tract polygons---require substantial
data engineering. As a result, researchers who could benefit from this dataset
are often unable to leverage it without considerable ad-hoc effort.

The \CRANpkg{cnefetools} package addresses this gap. It provides a unified
interface for downloading, caching, and analysing CNEFE data directly from R,
with four principal capabilities:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Data access}: download and read CNEFE data for any municipality,
    with transparent caching to avoid repeated downloads.
  \item \textbf{Address counts}: aggregate address points to H3 hexagonal grids
    or user-supplied polygons, returning counts by address type.
  \item \textbf{Land-use mix indices}: compute a suite of six established and
    novel land-use mix (LUM) indices, including the Bidirectional
    Global-centered Balance Index (BGBI) of \citet{pedreirajr2025}.
  \item \textbf{Dasymetric interpolation}: redistribute census tract aggregates
    to finer spatial units using CNEFE dwelling points as ancillary data.
\end{enumerate}

A key technical contribution is a dual-backend architecture in which the
default DuckDB engine \citep{duckdb2019} reads CSV data directly from cached
ZIP archives, performs H3 cell assignment and spatial joins in-process, and
returns results without intermediate file extraction. This design achieves
speedups of up to 13$\times$ over a pure-R fallback based on
\CRANpkg{h3jsr} and \CRANpkg{sf} (see Section 5 for benchmarks).

This paper is organised as follows. Section 2 describes the CNEFE dataset
and its distribution. Section 3 presents the architectural decisions
underpinning \pkg{cnefetools}. Section 4 describes each exported function in
detail. Section 5 reports performance benchmarks. Section 6 presents a
complete worked example. Section 7 summarises contributions and discusses
future directions.

## The CNEFE Dataset

The Cadastro Nacional de Endereços para Fins Estatísticos (CNEFE) is the
geocoded address register produced alongside the 2022 Brazilian Census. It was
designed to serve statistical---rather than postal---purposes, and as such it
records not only residences but also non-residential address points including
commercial establishments, agricultural units, schools, health facilities, and
religious institutions \citep{ibge_cnefe2022}.

### Structure

Each CNEFE record corresponds to a single address point. The dataset uses a
fixed schema of 34 columns. The most analytically relevant columns are:

- **`LONGITUDE`** and **`LATITUDE`**: geographic coordinates in the SIRGAS
  2000 datum (EPSG:4674, approximately equivalent to WGS 84 for most
  applications).
- **`COD_ESPECIE`**: an integer code (1--8) identifying the functional category
  of the address, referred to in this paper as the *address type*. The eight
  categories are listed in Table \ref{tab:especie}.
- **`COD_SETOR`**: the census tract code, linking each address to the
  corresponding tract-level aggregate statistics.
- Address description fields: street type (`TIP_LOGR`), street name
  (`NOM_LOGR`), number (`NUM_SIGLA`), complement, neighbourhood, and postal
  code (`CEP`).

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{CNEFE address types (\texttt{COD\_ESPECIE}).}
\label{tab:especie}
\begin{tabular}{cll}
\hline
\textbf{Code} & \textbf{English label} & \textbf{Portuguese label} \\
\hline
1 & Private household & Domicílio particular \\
2 & Collective household & Domicílio coletivo \\
3 & Agricultural establishment & Estabelecimento agropecuário \\
4 & Educational establishment & Estabelecimento de ensino \\
5 & Health establishment & Estabelecimento de saúde \\
6 & Other establishment & Estabelecimento de outras finalidades \\
7 & Under construction & Edificação em construção ou reforma \\
8 & Religious establishment & Estabelecimento religioso \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

### Scale and Distribution

The CNEFE covers all Brazilian municipalities. The number of address records
per municipality ranges from a few hundred in small rural municipalities to
approximately 5.7 million in the city of São Paulo, the most populous city
in the Southern Hemisphere. The full dataset comprises approximately 100
million records.

The data are distributed by IBGE via an FTP server at
\url{https://ftp.ibge.gov.br/Cadastro\_Nacional\_de\_Enderecos\_para\_Fins\_Estatisticos/}.
Files are organised hierarchically by Federative Unit (UF) and municipality.
Each municipality's data are stored as a semicolon-delimited CSV file
compressed inside a ZIP archive.

### Challenges for Programmatic Use

Working with the CNEFE presents several practical difficulties that
\pkg{cnefetools} is designed to overcome:

1. **Discovery**: there is no unified index of download URLs. Constructing
   the correct URL requires knowledge of the state acronym, the full
   municipality name as it appears in the FTP directory (which does not always
   match standard IBGE name fields), and the municipality code.

2. **Download size**: ZIP files range from a few kilobytes (small rural
   municipalities) to several hundred megabytes (large cities). Repeated
   downloads are impractical in interactive workflows.

3. **Parsing**: the CSV files are Latin-1-encoded and semicolon-delimited, and
   some columns are inconsistently typed across municipalities. Robust parsing
   requires explicit options.

4. **Spatial operations at scale**: assigning millions of point coordinates to
   hexagonal cells or performing point-in-polygon joins with census tract
   geometries is computationally intensive in pure R.

## Package Design

\pkg{cnefetools} addresses the challenges above through four key design
decisions: a pre-built URL index, a persistent user-level cache, a dual
DuckDB/R backend, and a layered set of DuckDB extensions.

### Pre-built Municipality Index

The package ships with an internal data frame (`cnefe_index`) that maps
each of Brazil's approximately 5,570 seven-digit IBGE municipality codes to
its corresponding CNEFE ZIP download URL. This index was constructed from the
FTP directory listing at the time of package release. The index also records
the municipality name and the Federative Unit (UF), enabling informative error
messages when an unrecognised code is provided.

Municipality codes are validated and normalised by the internal helper
`.normalize_code_muni()`, which accepts integer, character, and numeric inputs
and checks that the code is a seven-digit IBGE code present in the index.

### Persistent User-level Caching

ZIP files are cached in the user's application data directory, obtained via
`tools::R_user_dir("cnefetools", "cache")`. Once a municipality's ZIP file has
been downloaded, subsequent calls to any function that requires it---
`read_cnefe()`, `cnefe_counts()`, `compute_lumi()`, or
`tracts_to_h3()`---reuse the cached file without re-downloading. The cache
directory is created on first use and persists across R sessions.

Download integrity is verified by checking that the downloaded file is a valid
ZIP archive and that it contains a CSV file matching the expected naming
pattern. Corrupted or incomplete downloads are detected and retried with
increasing timeout thresholds (300 s, 600 s, and 1,800 s).

Caching can be disabled with `cache = FALSE`, in which case the ZIP is
written to a temporary file and removed when the function exits.

### Dual-backend Architecture

The most computationally intensive operations---assigning millions of
coordinates to H3 hexagons and performing spatial joins---are available with
two interchangeable backends, selectable via the `backend` argument:

- **`"duckdb"` (default)**: uses an in-memory DuckDB database with three
  extensions: `zipfs` (to read CSV directly from the cached ZIP),
  `h3` (for H3 cell assignment entirely in SQL), and `spatial` (for
  point-in-polygon joins). All computation occurs inside DuckDB; only
  the final aggregated result is transferred to R.

- **`"r"`**: uses \CRANpkg{arrow} to read the CSV, \CRANpkg{h3jsr} to compute
  H3 cell indices, and \CRANpkg{sf} for spatial joins. This backend is
  slower but requires no DuckDB extensions beyond the base `duckdb` R
  package.

The DuckDB backend leverages the `zipfs` community extension to read the CSV
directly from the ZIP archive without decompressing it to disk. This
is particularly valuable for large municipalities, where extraction alone
can take several seconds. Extensions are loaded lazily: \pkg{cnefetools}
checks whether each extension is already installed and loaded before
attempting installation, so the overhead on repeat calls is negligible.

Census tract geometry assets required by `tracts_to_h3()` and
`tracts_to_polygon()` are stored as Parquet files on GitHub Releases and
downloaded on-demand with caching via the \CRANpkg{piggyback} package.

### Input Validation

All user-facing functions validate their inputs using \CRANpkg{checkmate}
before any I/O or computation. Municipality codes are normalised and checked
against the built-in index. Polygon inputs are verified to be `sf` objects
with `POLYGON` or `MULTIPOLYGON` geometry types, and invalid geometries are
repaired with `sf::st_make_valid()`. CRS arguments are validated by attempting
`sf::st_crs()` on the user-supplied value.

## Core Functionality

### `read_cnefe()`: Reading CNEFE Data

`read_cnefe()` is the entry point to the package. It downloads (if not cached)
and reads the CNEFE CSV for a municipality, returning either an
\CRANpkg{arrow} `Table` or an \CRANpkg{sf} point object:

```{r read-cnefe-example}
library(cnefetools)

# Read as an Arrow table (default; no spatial conversion)
cnefe <- read_cnefe(code_muni = 2933307)
cnefe

# Read as an sf point object with SIRGAS 2000 coordinates (EPSG:4674)
cnefe_sf <- read_cnefe(code_muni = 2933307, output = "sf")
```

The `output = "arrow"` default (an `arrow::Table`) is preferred for large
municipalities because it avoids materialising the full data frame in memory.
The table can be filtered and projected using \CRANpkg{dplyr} verbs applied
to Arrow tables before conversion to a data frame. The `output = "sf"` option
converts the Arrow table to a data frame, coerces `LONGITUDE` and `LATITUDE`
to numeric, drops rows with missing coordinates, and constructs an `sf` point
object in CRS 4674 (SIRGAS 2000).

The function accepts the `year` argument (currently only 2022 is supported),
`cache` to control ZIP caching, and `verbose` to toggle informative progress
messages.

### `cnefe_counts()`: Address Counts on Spatial Units

`cnefe_counts()` aggregates CNEFE address points to spatial units and returns
counts of each address type (1 through 8) as columns `addr_type1` to
`addr_type8`:

```{r counts-hex-example}
# Count addresses per H3 hexagon at resolution 9
hex_counts <- cnefe_counts(
  code_muni = 2933307,
  h3_resolution = 9
)

# Count per user-supplied polygon (e.g., neighbourhoods)
library(geobr)
nei <- subset(read_neighborhood(year = 2022), code_muni == 2933307)

poly_counts <- cnefe_counts(
  code_muni   = 2933307,
  polygon_type = "user",
  polygon      = nei
)
```

When `polygon_type = "hex"` (default), the function builds a complete H3
grid over the municipality boundary using \CRANpkg{geobr} to obtain the
boundary polygon and \CRANpkg{h3jsr} (or the DuckDB `h3` extension) to
generate the hexagons. The H3 resolution can be set from 0 (coarse) to 15
(fine); the default is 9, corresponding to an average hexagon area of
approximately 105,000 m² and a diameter of roughly 400 m.

When `polygon_type = "user"`, the function performs a point-in-polygon join
between the CNEFE coordinates and the user-supplied `sf` polygon. A warning
is issued if any CNEFE points fall outside the polygon, together with the
coverage percentage; an error is raised if no points fall inside.

The output `sf` object inherits the polygon geometry: H3 hexagons when
`polygon_type = "hex"`, or the user-supplied polygons (in their original CRS,
or `crs_output` if specified) when `polygon_type = "user"`.

### `compute_lumi()`: Land-use Mix Indices

`compute_lumi()` computes a suite of six land-use mix (LUM) indices from
CNEFE address data. All indices are based on the local residential share
$p_i = n_{\text{res},i} / n_{\text{tot},i}$, where $n_{\text{res},i}$
is the count of type-1 addresses (private households) and $n_{\text{tot},i}$
is the total count of addresses of types 1--8, excluding type 7 (under
construction), within spatial unit $i$. The citywide residential share $P$
is computed from the full municipality CNEFE.

The six indices are:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Residential share} (\texttt{p\_res}): $p_i$, the proportion of
    addresses that are private households.
  \item \textbf{Entropy Index} (\texttt{ei}, \citealp{song2013}):
    $\text{EI}_i = -\bigl(p_i \ln p_i + q_i \ln q_i\bigr) / \ln 2$,
    where $q_i = 1 - p_i$. Ranges from 0 (homogeneous) to 1 (equal split).
  \item \textbf{Herfindahl-Hirschman Index} (\texttt{hhi}):
    $\text{HHI}_i = p_i^2 + q_i^2$. Ranges from 0.5 (equal split) to 1
    (homogeneous).
  \item \textbf{Balance Index} (\texttt{bal}, \citealp{booth2001}):
    $\text{BAL}_i = 1 - |p_i - \frac{P}{1-P} q_i| \,/\, (p_i +
    \frac{P}{1-P} q_i)$. Measures balance relative to the citywide
    composition; ranges from 0 to 1.
  \item \textbf{Index of Concentration at Extremes} (\texttt{ice}):
    $\text{ICE}_i = p_i - q_i$. Ranges from $-1$ (fully non-residential)
    to $+1$ (fully residential).
  \item \textbf{Adapted HHI} (\texttt{hhi\_adp}): a signed version of the
    scaled HHI, $\text{HHI\_adp}_i = \text{sign}(p_i - q_i) \cdot
    (\text{HHI}_i - 0.5) / 0.5$.
  \item \textbf{Bidirectional Global-centered Balance Index} (\texttt{bgbi},
    \citealp{pedreirajr2025}):
    $$\text{BGBI}_i = \frac{(2p_i - 1) - (2P - 1)}{1 - (2p_i - 1)(2P - 1)}$$
    Ranges from $-1$ to $+1$. Positive values indicate that the local
    residential share exceeds the citywide reference ($p_i > P$); negative
    values indicate non-residential dominance. Unlike EI and BAL, BGBI is
    both directional and globally referenced, addressing two key limitations
    of classical indices.
\end{itemize}

The function interface mirrors `cnefe_counts()`:

```{r lumi-example}
# Compute all six indices on H3 hexagons at resolution 9
lumi_hex <- compute_lumi(code_muni = 2933307)

# Compute on user-supplied polygons
lumi_poly <- compute_lumi(
  code_muni    = 2933307,
  polygon_type = "user",
  polygon      = nei
)
```

The output `sf` object contains columns `id_hex` (or the original polygon
columns), `p_res`, `ei`, `hhi`, `bal`, `ice`, `hhi_adp`, `bgbi`, and the
polygon geometry.

### `tracts_to_h3()` and `tracts_to_polygon()`: Dasymetric Interpolation

Census data in Brazil are published at the census tract level
(*setor censitário*). Census tracts are the smallest geographic unit for
which aggregate statistics are released, typically covering 200--400 private
households. For many applications, however, the desired spatial unit
differs from census tracts: researchers may want data on a regular H3 grid
for comparative analyses, or on custom polygons such as traffic zones for
transportation modelling.

`tracts_to_h3()` and `tracts_to_polygon()` perform dasymetric interpolation
\citep{mennis2003} from census tract aggregates to finer spatial units,
using CNEFE dwelling points as ancillary data. The procedure, carried out
entirely within DuckDB for efficiency, proceeds in four steps:

1. **Spatial join (tracts → CNEFE points)**: each CNEFE point (types 1 and 2
   only---private and collective dwellings) is matched to the census tract
   that contains it via `ST_Within`.
2. **Allocation**: the tract-level total for each requested variable is
   divided equally among eligible dwelling points within that tract. The
   eligibility rule depends on the variable: `pop_ph` (private household
   population) is allocated only to type-1 points; `pop_ch` to type-2;
   demographic variables (`male`, `female`, `age_*`, `race_*`) follow
   private dwellings when available, otherwise collective. For
   `avg_inc_resp` (average income of household heads), the tract-level
   average is assigned (not split) to each eligible point; the target unit
   receives the mean of assigned values.
3. **H3 / polygon aggregation**: allocated values are summed (or averaged for
   `avg_inc_resp`) within each target spatial unit.
4. **Diagnostics**: the function reports the percentage of the tract-level
   total successfully allocated, the percentage of CNEFE points matched to
   a tract, and any tracts with missing values.

```{r tracts-example}
# Interpolate population in private and collective households to H3 hexagons
hex_pop <- tracts_to_h3(
  code_muni    = 2933307,
  h3_resolution = 9,
  vars         = c("pop_ph", "pop_ch")
)

# Interpolate to user-supplied polygons (e.g., traffic zones)
poly_pop <- tracts_to_polygon(
  code_muni = 2933307,
  polygon   = my_zones,
  vars      = c("pop_ph", "avg_inc_resp")
)
```

The set of supported variables is documented in the `tracts_variables_ref`
reference table shipped with the package, which maps each variable name to the
official IBGE census tract code and a plain-language description.

## Performance

The DuckDB backend derives its performance advantage from three sources:

1. **In-process execution**: DuckDB runs as a library within the R process,
   eliminating the overhead of client–server communication.
2. **Reading directly from ZIP**: the `zipfs` extension exposes ZIP archives
   as virtual file systems, so DuckDB reads the compressed CSV without a
   separate extraction step.
3. **Vectorised query execution**: DuckDB uses a columnar engine with
   SIMD-accelerated operations, making aggregation and joins over tens of
   millions of rows substantially faster than row-oriented R operations.

Tables \ref{tab:bench-cities} and \ref{tab:bench-res} summarise the results
of a benchmark run on a machine with a 14-core Intel Core i9-13900H (2.60 GHz),
32 GB RAM, Windows 11, R 4.3.2, and DuckDB 1.4.1. Figure \ref{fig:bench}
shows the same data as bar charts.

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{Elapsed time (s) and DuckDB speedup for \texttt{cnefe\_counts()} at
         H3 resolution 8, by municipality size.
         Hardware: Intel Core i9-13900H (14 cores, 2.60 GHz), 32 GB RAM,
         Windows 11, R 4.3.2, DuckDB 1.4.1.}
\label{tab:bench-cities}
\begin{tabular}{llrrr}
\hline
\textbf{Municipality} & \textbf{Approx.\ addresses} &
  \textbf{Pure-R (s)} & \textbf{DuckDB (s)} & \textbf{Speedup} \\
\hline
Vitória da Conquista-BA & 200,000   & \textit{TODO} & \textit{TODO} & 1.53$\times$ \\
Curitiba-PR             & 900,000   & \textit{TODO} & \textit{TODO} & 4.40$\times$ \\
São Paulo-SP            & 5,700,000 & \textit{TODO} & \textit{TODO} & 13.08$\times$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\smallskip
\footnotesize \textit{Note}: For small municipalities, DuckDB incurs a fixed
initialisation overhead (connection setup, extension loading, data
materialisation) that can offset its query-execution gains, making the
pure-R backend competitive or faster. Fill in elapsed times (s) from the
bar chart labels in the \texttt{bench\_duckdb} vignette.
\end{table}

\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\caption{Elapsed time (s) and DuckDB speedup for \texttt{cnefe\_counts()} on
         Curitiba-PR, by H3 resolution.}
\label{tab:bench-res}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\hline
\textbf{H3 resolution} & \textbf{Avg.\ hex area (m²)} &
  \textbf{Pure-R (s)} & \textbf{DuckDB (s)} & \textbf{Speedup} \\
\hline
7  & 5,161,293 & \textit{TODO} & \textit{TODO} & 5.38$\times$ \\
9  &   105,333 & \textit{TODO} & \textit{TODO} & 3.46$\times$ \\
11 &     2,150 & \textit{TODO} & \textit{TODO} & 1.31$\times$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

\smallskip
\footnotesize At finer resolutions, H3 indexing dominates both backends
equally, reducing the relative DuckDB advantage. Fill in elapsed times (s)
from the bar chart labels in the \texttt{bench\_duckdb} vignette.
\end{table}

```{r fig-bench, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Performance comparison between DuckDB and pure-R backends. Top: elapsed time by municipality (H3 resolution 8). Bottom: elapsed time by H3 resolution (Curitiba-PR). DuckDB speedup is highest for large municipalities at coarser resolutions.\\label{fig:bench}"}
# TODO: Place the two benchmark bar charts (exported from the bench_duckdb
# vignette) as a combined figure in paper/figures/bench-backends.png,
# then change eval=FALSE to eval=TRUE below.

# knitr::include_graphics("figures/bench-backends.png")
```

The speedup increases with the number of address points. For large cities such
as São Paulo, the DuckDB backend reduces processing from several minutes to
seconds. For small municipalities (fewer than $\sim$50,000 addresses), the
DuckDB initialisation overhead can dominate and the two backends are comparable
in elapsed time.

The H3 resolution has a smaller effect on relative performance. At finer
resolutions (e.g., resolution 11, average hexagon area $\approx$2,150 m²),
the H3 point-to-cell indexing step dominates total runtime in both backends
equally, shrinking the relative DuckDB advantage.

The pure-R backend (`backend = "r"`) remains available for environments where
DuckDB extensions cannot be installed, such as some restricted computing
clusters. For most users, the default DuckDB backend is recommended.

## Worked Example

This section demonstrates a complete end-to-end workflow using
Vitória da Conquista (IBGE code 2933307), a mid-sized city in the state of
Bahia with approximately 200,000 CNEFE address points. The workflow illustrates
all four principal capabilities of \pkg{cnefetools} and runs from scratch
(without a pre-populated cache) in under ten minutes on a modern laptop.

### Step 1 — Reading CNEFE Data

```{r worked-step1, cache=TRUE}
library(cnefetools)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)

code_muni <- 2933307  # Vitória da Conquista-BA

# Download (first run) or read from cache (subsequent runs)
cnefe <- read_cnefe(code_muni = code_muni)
cnefe
#> Table
#> 205,412 rows x 34 columns
```

The returned Arrow table has approximately 205,000 rows (the exact count
reflects the 2022 Census enumeration) and 34 columns. The table can be
filtered and aggregated using standard `dplyr` verbs before conversion to an
R data frame.

### Step 2 — Address Counts on H3 Hexagons

```{r worked-step2, cache=TRUE}
# Aggregate all eight address types to H3 resolution 9 hexagons
hex_counts <- cnefe_counts(
  code_muni    = code_muni,
  h3_resolution = 9
)
hex_counts
#> Simple feature collection with 632 features
#> Geometry type: POLYGON
#> CRS:           EPSG:4326
#> # A tibble: 632 × 10
#>   id_hex           addr_type1 addr_type2 ... addr_type8 geometry
```

The result is an `sf` polygon object with one row per H3 hexagon that
intersects the municipality boundary. Hexagons with no CNEFE points receive
zero counts.

```{r fig-hex-counts, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Address counts per H3 hexagon (resolution 9) for Vitória da Conquista-BA. Each cell is shaded by the number of private households (\\texttt{addr\\_type1}).\\label{fig:hex-counts}"}
# TODO: Export the hex_counts map (e.g. using mapview or ggplot2) and save it
# as paper/figures/hex-counts.png, then change eval=FALSE to eval=TRUE below.

# knitr::include_graphics("figures/hex-counts.png")
```

### Step 3 — Land-use Mix: BGBI Map

```{r worked-step3, cache=TRUE}
# Compute all six LUM indices on H3 hexagons
lumi <- compute_lumi(
  code_muni    = code_muni,
  h3_resolution = 9
)

# Map the BGBI index
library(mapview)
mapview(
  lumi,
  zcol       = "bgbi",
  layer.name = "BGBI",
  col.regions = colorRampPalette(c("#2166AC", "white", "#D6604D"))(100)
)
```

The BGBI map reveals the spatial structure of residential vs.\ non-residential
land use relative to the citywide baseline. Values near zero indicate a
local residential share close to the citywide average; positive values
(blue) indicate predominantly residential cells; negative values (red)
indicate non-residential concentrations such as commercial districts,
industrial zones, or institutional campuses.

```{r fig-bgbi, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="BGBI map for Vitória da Conquista-BA at H3 resolution 9. Blue cells have a local residential share above the citywide average; red cells are dominated by non-residential uses.\\label{fig:bgbi}"}
# TODO: Export a static BGBI map (e.g. using ggplot2 + scale_fill_distiller) and
# save it as paper/figures/bgbi-vca.png, then change eval=FALSE to eval=TRUE below.

# knitr::include_graphics("figures/bgbi-vca.png")
```

### Step 4 — Dasymetric Interpolation of Census Population

```{r worked-step4, cache=TRUE}
# Interpolate population in private households to H3 hexagons
hex_pop <- tracts_to_h3(
  code_muni    = code_muni,
  h3_resolution = 9,
  vars         = c("pop_ph", "race_preta", "avg_inc_resp")
)

# Visualise population density
ggplot(hex_pop) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = pop_ph), colour = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    option = "C",
    name   = "Population\n(private HH)",
    trans  = "sqrt"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  labs(title = "Dasymetric population — Vitória da Conquista")
```

The function reports allocation diagnostics: the proportion of the
municipality's total census population that was successfully distributed to
CNEFE dwelling points and, subsequently, to H3 hexagons. In practice, the
vast majority of tract-level values are allocated, with minor losses due to
CNEFE points that fall outside all census tract polygons (typically fewer
than 1--2% of points).

```{r fig-pop, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Dasymetric population estimate per H3 hexagon (resolution 9) for Vitória da Conquista-BA, derived from the 2022 census tract totals via CNEFE dwelling points as ancillary data. Square-root colour scale.\\label{fig:pop}"}
# TODO: Export the population map (ggplot2 + scale_fill_viridis_c) and save it
# as paper/figures/pop-vca.png, then change eval=FALSE to eval=TRUE below.

# knitr::include_graphics("figures/pop-vca.png")
```

### Extending to Multiple Municipalities

The package is designed to be used within iteration workflows. The following
snippet, adapted from \citet{pedreirajr2026}, shows how to apply
`tracts_to_polygon()` to all 39 municipalities of the São Paulo Metropolitan
Region using `purrr::map()` and a set of traffic zone polygons from the
\CRANpkg{odbr} package:

```{r multi-muni, eval=FALSE}
library(purrr)
library(odbr)

# Download 2017 Origin-Destination survey zones for São Paulo Metro Region
zones <- read_od(city = "sao_paulo", year = 2017)

# Interpolate for all 39 municipalities iteratively
rmsp_pop <- map(
  unique(zones$code_muni),
  \(muni) tracts_to_polygon(
    code_muni = muni,
    polygon   = subset(zones, code_muni == muni),
    vars      = c("pop_ph", "avg_inc_resp")
  )
) |>
  bind_rows()
```

Because each municipality's ZIP is cached after the first download, re-running
this workflow (e.g., to add variables) reads from disk and completes in a
fraction of the original time.

## Summary

\pkg{cnefetools} makes the 2022 Brazilian CNEFE address dataset accessible and
analytically useful from R. Its principal contributions are:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Data access}: a pre-built municipality index and persistent
    caching layer that abstract away the FTP structure and eliminate repeated
    downloads.
  \item \textbf{Address aggregation}: flexible aggregation of address counts
    to H3 hexagonal grids or arbitrary polygons, supporting both a fast DuckDB
    backend and a portable pure-R fallback.
  \item \textbf{Land-use mix indices}: a complete implementation of six
    established and novel LUM indices, including the directional, globally
    referenced BGBI.
  \item \textbf{Dasymetric interpolation}: tract-to-hexagon and tract-to-polygon
    redistribution of census variables using CNEFE dwelling points as
    ancillary data, with per-variable allocation diagnostics.
  \item \textbf{DuckDB dual-backend}: a performance architecture that achieves
    up to 13$\times$ speedup for large municipalities by reading ZIP-compressed
    CSV files, assigning H3 cells, and performing spatial joins entirely within
    an in-process DuckDB engine.
\end{enumerate}

The package has several current limitations. It supports only the 2022 CNEFE
edition; support for earlier or future editions is contingent on IBGE
publishing data in a consistent format and is planned for future releases. The
land-use mix indices are restricted to a binary residential/non-residential
classification; multi-category extensions are the subject of ongoing
methodological work. The dasymetric functions (`tracts_to_h3()` and
`tracts_to_polygon()`) use the DuckDB backend exclusively; a pure-R fallback
for these functions is not currently available.

Future development directions include support for the 2010 CNEFE (when
available with sufficient geocoding coverage), spatial autocorrelation
diagnostics for LUM indices, and integration with the Brazilian Urban Areas
(Áreas de Concentração de População) classification for multi-city comparative
workflows.

\pkg{cnefetools} is available on CRAN and its development version on GitHub
(\url{https://github.com/pedreirajr/cnefetools}). Documentation and
pre-rendered vignettes are available at
\url{https://pedreirajr.github.io/cnefetools/}.

\bibliography{cnefetools}
